title: How Everything in a Medieval Castle Worked, from Its Moats to Its Dungeons
description:
in Architecture, History |   	April 2nd, 2026  Leave a Comment
	
			

	

Very few of us have ever set foot near a genôÙuine medieval casôÙtle, espeôÙci
body:
in Architecture, History |   	April 2nd, 2026  Leave a Comment
	
			

	

Very few of us have ever set foot near a genôÙuine medieval casôÙtle, espeôÙcialôÙly if we donãt hapôÙpen to live in Europe. Yet pracôÙtiôÙcalôÙly all of us still, here in the twenôÙty-first cenôÙtuôÙry, refer with some freôÙquenôÙcy to their comôÙpoôÙnents in our everyôÙday speech. When we invoke moats, drawôÙbridges, dunôÙgeons, and even catôÙaôÙpults, we almost always do so metaphorôÙiôÙcalôÙly ã assumôÙing weãre not active memôÙbers of a hisôÙtorôÙiôÙcal re-creôÙation sociôÙety ã yet we also have no probôÙlem seeôÙing them before our mindãs eye with what feels like perôÙfect clarôÙiôÙty. The difôÙfiôÙculôÙty comes if we attempt to inteôÙgrate all of those images, absorbed hapôÙhazôÙardôÙly from folk tales and popôÙuôÙlar culôÙture, into a funcôÙtionôÙing whole.
The fact of the matôÙter is that peoôÙple in the MidôÙdle Ages realôÙly did live and work in casôÙtles, and occaôÙsionôÙalôÙly had to defend them, or indeed attack them. Using a 3D-renôÙdered repliôÙca conôÙstructôÙed to reflect how those strucôÙtures were built in the fragôÙmentôÙed Europe of the eleventh through fourôÙteenth cenôÙturies after the fall of the CarôÙolinôÙgian Empire, the DeconôÙstructôÙed video aboveô explains everyôÙthing about how they worked in the span of about twenôÙty minôÙutes.
This tour begins with the barôÙbiôÙcan: not the celôÙeôÙbratôÙed BruôÙtalôÙist comôÙplex in LonôÙdon, but the exteôÙriôÙor forôÙtiôÙfied pasôÙsage ãdesigned to expose attackôÙers to defenôÙsive fire before they even reach the main gate.ã And it only gets hardôÙer for would-be casôÙtle capôÙtors from there.
ParaôÙpets with cutouts through which archers could fire their arrows, the moat that made underôÙminôÙing (a term comôÙmon enough in modôÙern lanôÙguage that few now recôÙogôÙnize its oriôÙgins) next to imposôÙsiôÙble, the drawôÙbridge that could be pulled up, the walls slantôÙed to repel batôÙterôÙing rams, the spiked portcullisôÙes that could be slammed down: these are just a few of the myrôÙiôÙad defensôÙes that made invadersã lives difôÙfiôÙcult ã and, in many casôÙes, short, espeôÙcialôÙly when ãmurôÙder holesã were involved. (Now thereãs a term just waitôÙing for incluôÙsion in our lexôÙiôÙcon.) The examôÙple conôÙstructôÙed here repôÙreôÙsents the zenith of casôÙtle design, the culôÙmiôÙnaôÙtion of an evoôÙluôÙtionôÙary process that began in the tenth cenôÙtuôÙry with a strucôÙture called the motte and baiôÙley: a term that, if you donãt already know it from othôÙer conôÙtexts, you probôÙaôÙbly just donãt do enough verôÙbal batôÙtle on the interôÙnet.
RelatôÙed ConôÙtent:
How To Build a 13th-CenôÙtuôÙry CasôÙtle, Using Only AuthenôÙtic Medieval Tools & TechôÙniques
Behold a 21st-CenôÙtuôÙry Medieval CasôÙtle Being Built with Only Tools & MateôÙriôÙals from the MidôÙdle Ages
The TechôÙnolôÙoôÙgy That Brought Down Medieval CasôÙtles and Changed the MidôÙdle Ages
How Medieval CatheôÙdrals Were Built WithôÙout SciôÙence, or Even MathôÙeôÙmatôÙics
The Roman ColosôÙseôÙum DeconôÙstructôÙed: 3D AniôÙmaôÙtion Reveals the HidôÙden TechôÙnolôÙoôÙgy That PowôÙered Romeãs Great AreôÙna
Based in Seoul,ô ColôÙinô Marshallô writes and broadôÙcasts on cities, lanôÙguage, and culôÙture. Heãs the author of the newsletôÙterô Books on Citiesô as well as the booksô ÚõçÙ šš§ õ¡šÏô (No SumôÙmaôÙrizôÙing Korea) andô KoreôÙan Newtro.ô FolôÙlow him on the social netôÙwork forôÙmerôÙly known as TwitôÙter atô @colinmarshall.

title: Discover the Retirement Home for Elderly Musicians Created by Giuseppe Verdi: Created in 1899, It Still Lives On Today
description:
in Life, Music |   	April 2nd, 2026  Leave a Comment
	
			

	

Among my works, the one I like best is the Home that I have had built in Milan for ac
body:
in Life, Music |   	April 2nd, 2026  Leave a Comment
	
			

	

Among my works, the one I like best is the Home that I have had built in Milan for accomôÙmoôÙdatôÙing old singers not favored by forôÙtune, or who, when they were young did not posôÙsess the virtue of savôÙing. Poor and dear comôÙpanôÙions of my life!ô 
ãGiuseppe VerôÙdi
Is there a remôÙeôÙdy for the isoôÙlaôÙtion of old age?
What about the jolôÙly fraôÙterôÙniôÙty and comôÙpetôÙiôÙtiveôÙness of an art colôÙlege dorm, as enviôÙsioned by opera comôÙposôÙer Giuseppe VerôÙdi?
ShortôÙly before his death, the comôÙposôÙer donatôÙed all royôÙalôÙties from his operas to the conôÙstrucôÙtion and adminôÙisôÙtraôÙtion of a luxôÙuôÙriôÙous retreat for retired musiôÙcians, designed by his librettistãs brothôÙer, archiôÙtect CamilôÙlo Boito.
ComôÙpletôÙed in 1899, Casa VerôÙdi still serves elderôÙly musiôÙcians todayãup to 60 at a time. ResôÙiôÙdents of Casa VerôÙdiô include alumôÙnae of the MetôÙroôÙpolôÙiôÙtan Opera and the RoyôÙal Opera House. Guests have worked alongôÙside such notaôÙbles as Chet BakôÙer and Maria Callas.
ComôÙpeôÙtiôÙtion for resôÙiôÙdenôÙtial slots is stiff. To qualôÙiôÙfy, one must have been a proôÙfesôÙsionôÙal musiôÙcian or music teacher. Those selectôÙed enjoy room, board, and medôÙical treatôÙment in addiôÙtion to, writes The New York Times,ô ãaccess to conôÙcerts, music rooms, 15 pianos, a large organ, harps, drum sets and the comôÙpaôÙny of their peers.ã MusiôÙcal proôÙgramôÙming is as conôÙstant as the fine view of Verdiãs grave.
DinôÙing tables are named in honôÙor of Verdiãs works. Those inclined to worôÙship do so in a chapel named for SanôÙta CecilôÙia, the patron saint of musiôÙcians.
PracôÙtice rooms are alive with the sound of music and critôÙiôÙcism. As Casa Verdiãs music therôÙaôÙpist told the FinanôÙcial Times, ãThey are very comôÙpetôÙiôÙtive: they are all priôÙma donôÙnas.ã
When memôÙoôÙry fails, resôÙiôÙdents can tune in to such docôÙuôÙmenôÙtaries as actor Dustin Hoffmanãs Toscaãs Kiss, below


	

Get a peek inside Verdiãs retireôÙment home for artists, comôÙpliôÙments of Urban SketchôÙers here.
Note: An earôÙliôÙer verôÙsion of this post appeared on our site in 2018.
RelatôÙed ConôÙtent:
How a Dutch ãDemenôÙtia VilôÙlageã Improves QualôÙiôÙty of Life with IntenôÙtionôÙal Design
Meet Nadia Boulanger, ãThe Most InfluôÙenôÙtial Teacher Since Socrates,ã Who MenôÙtored Philip Glass, Leonard BernôÙstein, Aaron CopôÙland, QuinôÙcy Jones & OthôÙer LegôÙends
How Music Can AwakôÙen Patients with Alzheimerãs and DemenôÙtia
Ayun HalôÙlôÙiôÙday is an author, illusôÙtraôÙtor, and theôÙater makôÙer in NYC.

title: Watch the Titanic and Lusitania Sink in Real Time: One Fast, One Slow
description:
in History |   	April 3rd, 2026  Leave a Comment
	
			

	

Asked to name famous shipôÙwrecks at a bar trivôÙia night, a fair few parôÙticôÙiôÙpants might
body:
in History |   	April 3rd, 2026  Leave a Comment
	
			

	

Asked to name famous shipôÙwrecks at a bar trivôÙia night, a fair few parôÙticôÙiôÙpants might think immeôÙdiôÙateôÙly of Pearl HarôÙbor, whether or not they can recall that it was the USS AriôÙzona bombed there. More firmôÙly withôÙin livôÙing memôÙoôÙry sits the SS Andrea Doria, though sheãs hardôÙly the culôÙturôÙal refôÙerôÙence she used to be. The wreck of the SS Edmund FitzgerôÙald passed its fiftiôÙeth anniverôÙsary just last year, which gave a boost to its rememôÙbrance, if mostôÙly byô GorôÙdon LightôÙfoot fans. There is, of course, theô Endurance, though the ship herôÙself has always been overôÙshadôÙowed by the efforts of her capôÙtain to get the whole crew home alive. The schooner HesôÙpeôÙrusô does come to mind as a parôÙticôÙuôÙlarôÙly unforôÙtuôÙnate vesôÙsel, perôÙhaps all the more so because she didôÙnãt actuôÙalôÙly exist.
NearôÙly everyôÙone at the bar is, of course, going to put down the RMS TitanôÙic first. Even before she received the James Cameron treatôÙment, that ãunsinkôÙableã ocean linôÙer was easôÙiôÙly the most famous shipôÙwreck of the twenôÙtiôÙeth cenôÙtuôÙry, and quite posôÙsiôÙbly of all hisôÙtoôÙry. But secôÙond place has to go to the RMS LusiôÙtaôÙnia, which went under just three years after theô TitanôÙic. As close as the year 1915 may sound to 1912, develôÙopôÙments in Europe had rearranged the world in the meanôÙtime. The TitanôÙic met her end by colôÙlidôÙing with an iceôÙberg, and about two and a half hours latôÙer, as you can see in the real-time sinkôÙing video at the top of the post, it was on the botôÙtom of the North Atlantic. When the LusiôÙtaôÙniaô was torôÙpeôÙdoed by a GerôÙman Uãboat, by conôÙtrast, she went down in just eighôÙteen minôÙutes.
You can witôÙness those minôÙutesô re-creôÙatôÙed in the aniôÙmatôÙed video from OceanôÙlinôÙer Designs just above. Though the Great War was ragôÙing, the ship hadôÙnãt yet been comôÙmisôÙsioned as an armed merôÙchant cruisôÙer, but was conôÙductôÙing her usuôÙal transatôÙlantic pasôÙsenôÙger serôÙvice while ã as the GerôÙman side insistôÙed and the British at first denied ã carôÙryôÙing war mateôÙriôÙals on the side. Sheãd been travôÙelôÙing due east for six days when Uã20ô sightôÙed her; after an hour of trackôÙing came the launch of the fateôÙful underôÙwaôÙter misôÙsile and its 160-kiloôÙgram exploôÙsive payôÙload. The video shows and explains not just how the LusiôÙtaôÙnia slipped below the water, but also the breakôÙdown along the way of her varôÙiôÙous strucôÙturôÙal eleôÙments and mechanôÙiôÙcal sysôÙtems, includôÙing the eleôÙvaôÙtors that had once seemed such marôÙvelous innoôÙvaôÙtions.


	

It seems that after the torôÙpeôÙdo hit, pracôÙtiôÙcalôÙly everyôÙthing that could have conôÙseôÙquentôÙly gone wrong did, right down to the few deployôÙable lifeboats dropôÙping catôÙaôÙstrophôÙiôÙcalôÙly from their davits. The crew of the TitanôÙic manôÙaged to launch most of her lifeboats, but there werenãt enough of them in the first place. That conôÙtributed to a final death toll of aroundô 1,500, as comôÙpared with 1,197 on theô LusiôÙtaôÙnia. Though simôÙiôÙlar in scale and hisôÙtorôÙiôÙcal timôÙing, these two marôÙitime disôÙasôÙters endôÙed up with very difôÙferôÙent meanôÙings. The wreck of the TitanôÙic conôÙtinôÙues to capôÙture imagôÙiôÙnaôÙtions by resôÙonatôÙing with the indusôÙtriôÙal romance, class stratôÙiôÙfiôÙcaôÙtion, and impeôÙrôÙiôÙal hubris of the long nineôÙteenth cenôÙtuôÙry; that of the LusiôÙtaôÙnia, whose sinkôÙing played a major role in bringôÙing the UnitôÙed States into what we now call World War I, shows us nothôÙing so clearôÙly as the merôÙciôÙless geopoôÙlitôÙiôÙcal logôÙic of the twenôÙtiôÙeth.
RelatôÙed conôÙtent:
Watch the SinkôÙing of the LusiôÙtaôÙnia AniôÙmatôÙed in Real Time (1915)
How James Cameron Shot TitanôÙicãs HugeôÙly ComôÙplex SinkôÙing Scene
The SinkôÙing of the BriôÙtanôÙnic: An AniôÙmatôÙed IntroôÙducôÙtion to the Titanicãs ForôÙgotôÙten SisôÙter Ship
The CosôÙta ConôÙcorôÙdia ShipôÙwreck Viewed from OutôÙer Space
The First Full 3D Scan of the TitanôÙic, Made of More Than 700,000 Images CapôÙturôÙing the Wreckãs Every Detail
A New 3D Scan, CreôÙatôÙed from 25,000 High-ResôÙoôÙluôÙtion Images, Reveals the RemarkôÙably Well-PreôÙserved Wreck of Shackletonãs Endurance
Based in Seoul,ô ColôÙinô Marshallô writes and broadôÙcasts on cities, lanôÙguage, and culôÙture. Heãs the author of the newsletôÙterô Books on Citiesô as well as the booksô ÚõçÙ šš§ õ¡šÏô (No SumôÙmaôÙrizôÙing Korea) andô KoreôÙan Newtro.ô FolôÙlow him on the social netôÙwork forôÙmerôÙly known as TwitôÙter atô @colinmarshall.

title: A Line of Mural Wallpapers from Astek Celebrates 'Eterna Nouveau'
description:
All images courtesy of Astek, shared with permission
April 2, 2026


Living in a high-rise apartment or a house with a small yard comes with the disa
body:
All images courtesy of Astek, shared with permission
April 2, 2026


Living in a high-rise apartment or a house with a small yard comes with the disadvantage of not having access to garden space. Fortunately, fine wallpaper manufacturer Astek has a way to bring beautiful blooms indoors. The companyãs collection of dreamy floral mural designs called Eterna Nouveau reinterprets the Art Nouveau movement of the early 20th century, which historically flourished in Europe and emphasized nature-inspired motifs like flowers and birds.
Eterna Nouveauãs arching, sinuous stems and leaves nod to its namesake styleãs characteristic ãwhiplashã lines. ãAquavita,ã for example, features lilies and other water plants and illustrates life both above and below the surface. And ãCarnivoriaã celebrates more unusual plants, like Venus flytraps. A variety of colorways emphasizes the designsã bold forms and delicate metallic outlines.

The motifs were first created by hand, then digitized so that they can be sized up or down to fit custom spaces. Printed to order, the colors and shapes can even be customized for special projects. See more on Astekãs Instagram.

schema.headline: A Line of Mural Wallpapers from Astek Celebrates 'Eterna Nouveau'

title: Symbiotic Communion Flourishes in Laura Berger's Expansive Paintings
description: Chicago-based artist Laura Berger continues her explorations of communion in a suite of staggering paintings that place her signature minimal figu
body:
Chicago-based artist Laura Berger continues her explorations of communion in a suite of staggering paintings that place her signature minimal figures in intimate fellowship with one another and the earth. Spanning six feet wide, the monumental works layer limbs and landscapes, as nude bodies merge with waves, flowers, and sun-strewn clouds. 
Berger frequently gestures toward past experiences and the spiritual realm, particularly focusing on the ways time fogs the clarity of memory and how transformation emerges from myriad circumstances. This body of work expands on the themes of interdependence, too, as she renders her figures in varying states of translucence as they swim through a cresting wave or lounge atop pink petals.
ãThe Skyã (2026), oil on canvas, 38 x 72 inches
These pieces will be on view from April 17 to May 23 at Kristin Hjellegjerde Galleryô in West Palm Beach. Until then, explore more of the artistãs work on Instagram.
Detail of ãWomen Living Peacefully Under The Mountainã (2026)
ãSpring Tideã (2026), oil on canvas, 38 x 72 inches
ãWe Are Sleeping In The Gardenã (2026), oil on canvas, 38 x 72 inches
Detail of ãSpring Tideã (2026)

schema.headline: Symbiotic Communion Flourishes in Laura Berger's Expansive Paintings

title: Yamamoto Masao's Otherworldly Portraits Introduce Us to Expressive Owls
description: Through atmospheric, black-and-white photographs, Yamamoto Masao explores the emotional connections between image and memory. His intimate, otherw
body:
Through atmospheric, black-and-white photographs, Yamamoto Masao explores the emotional connections between image and memory. His intimate, otherworldly gelatin silver prints evoke dreamlike archival footage that has been somehow unyoked from the normal rhythms of time. His subjects vary, although he often focuses on landscapes and natural subjects, including a number of owls that roost in trees near his home in Japan.
Ten Owls at Yancey Richardson marks the artistãs seventh solo exhibition with the gallery, showcasing intimate portraits of the nocturnal birds. No larger than 10 inches on the longest side, these images are intended to be viewed up close in a way that brings these elusive creatures much nearer to us than we ever experience in the wild.
ãUntitled #1672 (from Kawa = Flow)ã (2016), gelatin silver print, 10 x 6 1/4 inches
Often peering directly at the camera, their alert, sage, sometimes wary or candid looks evoke a sense of emotional connection. Each avian is an expressive protagonist, with the deep blacks and soft edges of Yamamotoãs prints inviting reflection and empathy.
Over time, the impact of development in the countryside where Yamamoto lives has drawn his attention to these winged, woodland denizens. ãOwls have always been a familiar presence to me,ã Yamamoto says. ãHowever, as the number of people living in the forest grew, those with no interest in the laws of nature began clearing the trees. Sadly, the owlsã perches are vanishing. Even so, when I hear their faint hooting echoing from somewhere in the distance, I feel a sense of peace.ã
Ten Owls opens on April 16 and continues through May 22 in New York. See more on Masaoãs Instagram.
ãUntitled #1719 (from Kawa = Flow)ã (2024), gelatin silver print, 9 1/2 x 6 inches

title: Brushstrokes Transform into Beaded Topographies in Liza Lou's Mixed-Media Paintings
description: One of the many reasons artists like Lee Krasner, Jackson Pollock, Franz Kline, Cy Twombly, and other mid-20th-century pioneers of painterly abstr
body:
One of the many reasons artists like Lee Krasner, Jackson Pollock, Franz Kline, Cy Twombly, and other mid-20th-century pioneers of painterly abstraction were so innovative for their time is the use of the deliberate yet loose brushstroke. Pollock intuitively dribbled and splattered paint on surfaces spread across the floor of his studio, and Kline created bold, monochromatic paintings with just a few deceptively simple, gestural strokes of a large brush. Itãs this visceral approach to visual rhythms and color that continues to awe us today. (A major retrospective highlighting both Krasner and Pollockãs work is slated for The Met later this year.)
For artist Liza Lou, the calculation of brushstrokes, color, and gesture opens the door to another media type altogetherãbeads. The artist is known for using the material, including a large-scale installation titled ãKitchen,ã which took five years to create. In her recent work, she adds thousands of the diminutive baubles in myriad colors, shapes, and sizes to sweeps of oil paint on canvas. Tapping into the legacy of Abstract Expressionism, Lou parses the relationship between gesture, intention, organic forms, and the brushstroke as a subject unto itself.
Detail of ãEnjambmentã
Louãs works appear this month in FAQ, a solo exhibition at Thaddaeus Ropac. The title references questions that the artist returns to again and again in her practice. When does a painting become not a painting? Can a brushstroke be more than a brushstroke? ãThese works are about amplificationãabout making things more ideal,ã Lou says. ãThereãs a poem by Fernando Pessoa where he writes about wanting flowers to be more flowers than flowers, and in this body of work Iãm using my material as a way to make paint more paint than paint.ã
Unlike a quick swipe of a brush, each bead is meticulously placed amid a field of others, creating a chromatic topography. Lou likens them to painting ãstraight-out-of-the-tube,ã except that they canãt be mixed on the canvas. She relies on color relationships, textures, and precise placement to give the impression that, from a distance, the loose strokes and splatters have blended or merged. When viewed up close, we see distinct, saturated topographies that, in a rather macro sense, are delightfully sculptural with the soft ground of painted details underneath. ãMy process involves this improv where every stroke requires everything I have, my full attention,ã Lou says. ãEvery mark becomes this kind of violin-crescendo-holy shit-experience.ã
FAQ opens on April 10 and continues through May 23 in London. See more on Louãs Instagram.
ãOnomatopoeiaã (2026), oil paint and glass beads on stretched canvas, 52 x 51 x 1.75 inches
Detail of ãOnomatopoeiaã
ãAnalepsisã (2025), oil paint and glass beads on stretched canvas, 42.75 x 41.75 x 1.75 inches
Detail of ãAnalepsisã
ãStanzaã (2025), oil paint and glass beads on. stretched canvas, 52 x 51 x 1.75 inches
Detail of ãStanzaã
ãEcphonesisã (2026), oil paint and glass beads on stretched canvas, 42.75 x 41.75 x 1.75 inches
